text:
Background: Diabetic nephropathy (DN) remains one of the most severe complications of diabetes mellitus, necessitating the discovery of effective therapeutic interventions. While rhein has shown promise in treating DN, the precise molecular mechanisms underlying its efficacy remain unclear. This study investigated the protective role of rhein and its impact on the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling pathway.

Methods: We utilized a high glucose (HG)-induced podocyte injury model and a streptozocin (STZ)-induced DN mouse model, both treated with rhein. Cell viability was assessed via CCK-8 assays, and reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels were quantified using flow cytometry. We employed RT-qPCR to measure the expression of Ras-related C3 botulinum toxin substrate 1 (Rac1), NADPH Oxidase 1 (NOX1), and β-catenin. Furthermore, immunofluorescence (IF) was used to characterize glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4), α-smooth muscle actin (α-SMA), Nephrin, and Podocin. Renal morphology and tubulointerstitial fibrosis were evaluated through HE and Masson staining, while immunohistochemistry (IHC) and specialized biochemical kits were used to analyze protein expression and metabolic markers.

Results: Rhein treatment significantly attenuated HG-induced oxidative stress, iron accumulation (Fe2+), and the expression of ferroptosis markers (TFR1, ACSL4) and mesenchymal markers (α-SMA, Vimentin, Snail, and Desmin). We found that rhein effectively suppressed the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin axis. Genetic silencing of Rac1 mirrored the protective effects of rhein by reducing MDA, Fe2+, and downstream target expression. Conversely, Rac1 overexpression exacerbated podocyte injury by inhibiting SOD, GSH, and GPX4 synthesis while downregulating SLC7A11 and Nephrin. In vivo, lentiviral-mediated Rac1 overexpression significantly promoted ferroptosis and fibrotic markers, reversing the therapeutic benefits observed with rhein.

Conclusions: Our findings demonstrate that rhein attenuates diabetic nephropathy by inhibiting ferroptosis and the epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT). These protective effects are mediated through the regulation of the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling axis, highlighting rhein as a potential therapeutic agent for managing DN.
